name: Route new orders with Shopify and Google Sheets end to end

parameters.text: =✅ Route new orders with Shopify and Google Sheets end to end — run completed at {{ $now.toFormat('HH:mm') }}

parameters.content:
### Receive Inbound Webhook

Entry point: listens for POST calls on `/wf09145-order`. Use the TEST url while building, switch callers to the PRODUCTION url after publishing.

Next: Map to Canonical Schema

### Map to Canonical Schema

Shapes the item: writes `orderId`, `kind`, `payload`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202)

### Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202)

Sends the HTTP 202 response back to the caller — keep this fast; heavy work happens after/async.

Next: Route by Event Kind

### Route by Event Kind

Routes each item to one of 3 lanes: order.paid, order.cancelled + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Handle 'order.paid' → Google Sheets · ②→ Handle 'order.cancelled' → Shopify · ③→ Log Unrecognized Kind

### Handle 'order.paid' → Google Sheets

Upserts a row into 'Order Tracker', matched on `orderId` — re-running with the same key updates the row instead of duplicating it.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

### Handle 'order.cancelled' → Shopify

Writes to Shopify (upserts the matching order/customer record) — the commerce system of record for this pipeline.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Shopify access token

### Log Unrecognized Kind

Appends a new row to 'Order Tracker' — a human-readable audit trail that doesn't require opening n8n to inspect.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

### Consolidate Handling

Rejoins 3 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Save Execution Metadata

### Save Execution Metadata

Saves key fields onto the execution record — searchable later in n8n's execution list.

Next: Guard Non-Empty email

### Guard Non-Empty email

Drops items that fail the condition; only qualifying items continue (no false branch).

Next: Tag Pipeline Metadata

### Tag Pipeline Metadata

Shapes the item: writes `pipeline`, `version`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Summarize Run Stats

### Summarize Run Stats

Shapes the item: writes `runAt`, `itemCount`, `status`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Append Audit Row (Google Sheets)

### Append Audit Row (Google Sheets)

Appends a new row to 'Order Tracker' — a human-readable audit trail that doesn't require opening n8n to inspect.

Ports: ①→ Send Summary to Slack · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

### Send Summary to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#orders`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Outlook

### Escalate Failure to Outlook

Sends an email via Microsoft Outlook — recipient comes from the item's `email` field.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Microsoft OAuth2

# Route new orders with Shopify and Google Sheets end to end

**Pattern:** Order Fulfillment · **Level:** Advanced · **Trigger:** Webhook

## What it does
Route new orders with Shopify and Google Sheets end to end. It fires the moment an external system calls its webhook.

## Why it exists
Manual inventory is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Receive Inbound Webhook → Map to Canonical Schema → Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202) → Route by Event Kind → Handle 'order.paid' → Google Sheets → Consolidate Handling → Save Execution Metadata → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to order fulfillment without manual effort
- Anyone automating inventory in e-commerce
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Keeps your systems in sync
- Runs 24/7 in the background
- Saves hours of manual work each week

## Before first run
Connect credentials: Google OAuth2 / service account; Microsoft OAuth2; Shopify access token; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

The optional HMAC check runs before any business logic touches the payload — bad signatures are rejected at the door rather than after work has already been done with untrusted data. Acking fast (HTTP 202) and doing the real work after the response is what stops the sender's webhook delivery from timing out and re-delivering the same event.

## Practice exercises

You've got the basics covered — push further:

1. Turn on signature verification and trace what a tampered payload does at the 'Signature Valid?' gate.
2. Add a new event kind to the switch and give it its own handler.
3. Remove the fast-ack step and observe how response time changes under a slow downstream write.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## 🔐 Security

Never trust inbound payloads: the webhook path is unguessable. Add header auth or IP allow-listing for defense in depth. Secrets live in n8n credentials — never in node parameters.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your inventory process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
